linkedin:
June fills every allied health appointment book.

And most practice owners love it.

The private health fund extras rush brings familiar faces back through the door, new enquiries from patients who have been meaning to book all year, and revenue figures that look strong when you pull the end-of-month report.

Here is the pattern that deserves a closer look before the rush hits.

The work performed in June is structurally different from the work performed in March. When demand spikes across all patient types, appointment books tend to fill with shorter, rebatable consultations rather than the extended assessments and complex sessions that carry the best margin. The practice is at capacity. The margin per appointment has quietly compressed.

There is a timing problem sitting behind the revenue number too. Wages for the extended June roster are paid immediately. Health fund reimbursements process within a few days for most funds, but gap payments, HICAPS reconciliations, and any concurrent Medicare or NDIS billing create administrative volume that pushes a portion of the cash into July. Then the superannuation contributions for April to June fall due. The appointment diary drops mid-July as the surge subsides. The practice is carrying June-level costs with a quieter book.

None of this means June is a bad month. It means June rewards preparation.

The practices that come out of the extras rush with genuine profitability, not just strong revenue, tend to do three things before June begins: they decide which item-number mix to fill their calendar with first, they tighten their claiming documentation before volume accelerates, and they build a forward cash flow view that covers the June-to-July transition before the crunch arrives.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#CashFlow #AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
facebook:
June is usually the favourite month for allied health practices. The private health fund extras rush is real, and appointment books fill quickly.

But there is a question worth asking before the rush hits: is your June revenue actually as profitable as it looks?

The extras surge changes your item mix, your staffing costs, and your cash flow timing in ways that only become clear in July. The practices that manage this well decide in advance which items to fill their calendar with, tighten their claiming process before the volume arrives, and build a cash view that covers the post-surge transition.

If your June is looking full, it might be worth checking whether the margin is keeping pace with the bookings.

What does June usually look like for your practice? Read more at profit-pulse.com.au/blog-post

#AlliedHealth #SMEFinance
